I'm definitely +1 on this. However, when it comes to the implementation, there are some sneaky details. I was actually trying to implement such thing with a "soft delete" approach, similar to what they do in [asyncio.Semaphore](https://github.com/python/asyncio/blob/master/asyncio/locks.py#L420), but I struggled to get it right though.

Sure, we can use linked lists, but this [won't actually give as any performance boost](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/8176513/ordereddict-performance-compared-to-deque), since `deque` is written in C, and will outperform any smart data structure written in python, unless we have asymptotically large number of waiters in a queue, which seems highly unlikely. 

So I would try to stick with the "soft delete approach" right now.

> Would need to think about the oneshot option. One potential problem with
> I/O is that Curio could just sit there forever waiting for something to happen.
> That's probably not what you want if you're interfacing Curio with something else.

Well, once we step outside the ideal world of curio, we are faced with the very classical
problem of "To block, or not-to block, how to block, when to block...". Infact curio is
the final result of collective iterations of finding a solution to this. Look at those huge
pile of code trying to find the right mix. Yes, the place curio is standing is the result
of too much sweat and blood. And that nobody's fault.

Anyway, that said, there are too many ways for dealing the blocking problem.
But I think we should not. We can always say "Go and get your fancy main
coordinator coroutine". And at the last resort thread are always there.

Saying this probably easy, because the developer who leaves child coros behind is
either knows what he is doing or doesn't know what he is doing. In either case he
deserves the same answer. ;)

I think we should focus on providing options for
- Cases can NOT be covered coordinator coro. "Oneshot" seems to be one of them. Or is it?
- Timers can easily be covered. May be providing a shortcut is handy. But I'm not sure.
- What else?

Lets put all those really necessary cases and decide for the final parameters for run().

We probably dont want to contribute to the electricity bill of people running curio
main run loop having certain "if conditions" evaluating false for 99.99999% of the time. ;)

Should 0 as a dimensionless quantity behave as a wild card? A dimensioned number plus a non-dimensioned number--even if that non-dimensioned number is an identity--doesn't make sense to me. I think distinguishing between a dimensioned and dimensionless 0 makes the most sense.

`(1 * ureg.mm) + (0 * ureg.mm)`

If it's inconvenient for users to deal with adding a number to a quantity where the number might be zero and where the dimensions were dropped, then they could always copy the units from the other quantity:

    q = 1 * ureg.mm
    r = 0
    q + (r * q.units)

But I don't think this would be needed with dimensional 0 and users' consistently carry units through their calculations (which they would need to do for non-zero values anyways).

Yeah, I noticed that when I dove into the code. lxml then calls html5lib's `parse` method and sets the `useChardet` flag to `True`. It appears that html5lib guesses the encoding incorrectly when a file is sent in, versus sending in a string. The `parse` method in `html5lib` does support an explicit argument for the encoding, but unfortunately `lxml` doesn't use that. I was going to suggest that it would be nice if you could pass in the encoding to `PyQuery`, but it looks like there's not much you would be able to do with it anyway, since lxml is not going to send it in to html5lib.

~~~

`alchy.Manager.reflect` doesn't support the `only` argument. That's something that's supported in SQLAlchemy's reflect() method but alchy doesn't curry that argument to it unfortunately. So you would need to extend `Manager`:

At the moment, dynamically updating markers is not supported. I agree that it would be a really good addition. I expect I'll get round to implementing this eventually, but till then, PRs welcome!

For anyone wanting to implement this, I think we should:
- move logic currently in `_marker_layer_options` into traitlets in the `Markers` widget. See for instance PR #211 that did the same for heatmaps. We need to preserve the current functionality, so the style traitlets (`hover_text`, `label`, `info_box_content` and `display_info_box`) will have to accept either a single argument that is broadcast to all markers, or a list, with one element per marker. We will probably have to use `validate` extensively at the `Markers` level.
- react to changes in the markers list in the frontend. See e.g. [the implementation for heatmaps](https://github.com/pbugnion/gmaps/blob/master/js/src/Heatmap.js#L81) for how to do this.

Okay, I think, I understood now what's going on.

Out of a habit, I called `pip install -e .`, which installs the package, but in `develop` mode.
Which is exactly the way I install packages on my system, when I plan to maybe work on them.

one can find more information about the development mode in [the setuptools docu](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#development-mode) 

I think it is reasonable to assume when `pip install .` works, 
so does `pip install -e .`.

At the moment you do this 
~~~python
try:
    from distutils.command.build_py import build_py_2to3 as build_py
except ImportError:
    from distutils.command.build_py import build_py
~~~
to find out if the user is building on py2 or py3, but the development mode needs to be aware as well to function properly, so by getting rid of that `try ... except` and instead doing
~~~python
setup(name="fitsio", 
     # ....
      use_2to3=True,
      cmdclass = {
        "build_ext": build_ext_subclass}
     )
~~~
you gain "pip -e"-installability.

In that case, `check_config_mode()` needs to be removed from `SSHConnection()` and placed in `BaseSSHConnection()`.  It's an extremely generic method that accepts an argument for the correct string to check and can thus be used by any vendor without rewriting the method in every vendor's specific class.

If you really wanted end users to be able to just call `device.check_config_mode()`, then you could override the method and return a `super()` call to the parent class.  I don't think it's too much to ask end users to use the method correctly, though, and call the parent class's `check_config_mode()` properly by specifying the expected string (`device.check_config_mode(check_string="]")`, for example).  That would deduplicate code and remove the need for an overridden method in `JuniperSSH`.  It would also remove it from `HPComwareSSH` and `SSHConnection`, two places where it already exists.

I'm just hoping O(n) `__getitem__` can be avoided somehow.  Ideally what you'd want is a way to ask a set / frozen set "at what index did you store this object?".  One way this could be achieved would be to implement a hashmap on top of a tuple in pure python, as bizarre as that sounds.

I took a little stab at this yesterday, also because cuckoo hashing is something I've been interested in for awhile.  However, the implementation is currently buggy and this may be a crazy thing to do in Python regardless:

```python
def cuckoo_insert(x, a):
    pos = hash(x) % len(a)
    if a[pos] is _EMPTY:
        a[pos] = x
        return
    # not sure if this is the best approach?
    pos = pos * (id(a) % len(a)) % len(a)
    if a[pos] is _EMPTY:
        a[pos] = x
        return
    evicted, a[pos] = a[pos], x
    print 'evicted', evicted, 'from', pos
    cuckoo_insert(evicted, a)
    # fingers crossed no infinite loop


def cuckoo_index(x, a):
    pos = hash(x) % len(a)
    if a[pos] == x:
        return pos
    pos = pos * (id(a) % len(a)) % len(a)
    if a[pos] == x:
        return pos
    raise ValueError('{} not in list'.format(x))


def cuckoo_extend(a, b):
    olda = [e for e in a if e is not _EMPTY]
    b = [e for e in b if e is not _EMPTY]
    a[:] = [_EMPTY] * (len(a) + len(b))
    for x in olda + b:
        cuckoo_insert(x, a)


_EMPTY = object()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    init = [_EMPTY] * 100
    for i in range(60) + list('cat'):
        cuckoo_insert(i, init)
    print init```

The way this might work is:

```python
class frozendict(tuple):
   def __init__(self, _dict):
      keys = cuckoo_extend([_EMPTY] * len(_dict) * 3 / 2, _dict)
      values = [_EMPTY] * len(keys)
      for k in _dict:
          values[cuckoo_index(k, keys)] = _dict[k]
      tuple.__init__(self, (tuple(keys), tuple(values)))

   def __getitem__(self, key):
      try:
         ix = cuckoo_index(key, self[0]):
      except ValueError:
         raise KeyError()
      return  self[1][ix]
```

Which is doing `cuckoo_insert()` for every item in the dict!  (Speaking of bad performance right...).

Hi, in short, no, TinyDB cannot natively handle datasets larger than RAM. However you can write your own storage that returns lazy dictionaries which only read more blocks of data as required. If you're at that stage however I believe the documentation encourages you to move on to more "proper" databases. These links may help:

 - [`prudent`](https://github.com/eugene-eeo/prudent) - small collection of lazy data structures
 - [UnQLite](https://unqlite.org) - an embeddable NoSQL database.
 - [unqlite-python](https://unqlite-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) - Python client for UnQLite.

Hi @ewallace - 

The behavior of `merge` is dependent on the installed version of BEDTools, and this behavior has changed in recent versions (see https://github.com/arq5x/bedtools2/issues/607). While it would be possible (and convenient) for pybedtools to detect on the fly whether or not a field contains a strand-like character, I would prefer not to do this since it would be too much of a performance hit in the general case. I think it's reasonable to assume correctly-formatted BED files when accessing by named attribute. That is, intervals encoding strand information to be accessed by the `.strand` attribute should be at least BED6 (chrom, start, stop, name, score, strand) and have the strand in the 6th column. It so happens `bedtools merge` from the version of BEDTools you have installed does not output correctly-formatted BED files when run in stranded mode.

That said, you can use

```python
myMergedTool[0][3]
```
to access the strand information encoded in the 4th field using integer indexing rather than named attributes.

Good writeup!

> My proposal would be to defined traits of transports and let content services specify which transport traits they require (and perhaps some user configuration).

:+1: Exactly my thought when I read through your itemization of transport selection issues.

A transport selector which receives ``requester`` (the object which requests a transport, usually another aioxmpp.Service, e.g. aioxmpp.FileTransferService), ``traits`` (a set of required transport traits), and ``not_traits`` (a set of forbidden traits) and returns a set of transport classes seems fine! (Normally, the selector would purely operate on the ``traits`` and ``not_traits`` arguments, but a user-defined selector could make use of the ``requester``, too.)

The transport classes have a set of traits themself. The default selector simply picks those which have all requested traits and none of the forbidden traits.

Maybe the transports should have weights, too, so that encrypted transports can be preferred over unencrypted transports (if they’re not forbidden which would make sense for e.g. for ZRTP payloads). Then the selector could return a sequence of transport classes in order of preference.

> We also have to ask what is within the scope of aioxmpp and what is not (and should e.g. instead be implemented by users of aioxmpp like jclib) – handling of audio/video streams is certainly out of scope (but negotiating a transport and providing data sources and sinks is probably not).

I *think* that aioxmpp should be able to give the user fully connected streams, but not care about the data which is transferred. aioice will probably help with that.

It should also allow the user to negotiate (using the signalling channel which is Jingle) the data which is transferred (i.e. codecs for A/V streams etc.).

> which sessions should be accepted and under what circumstances (in many cases user interaction may be necessary to determine whether to accept an action)

To handle this type of decision, I suggest to create a class which has methods which do that. For example, ``async def session_accept(self, sender, parameters)``. This also allows inheritance between different policies for different purposes. If the object is only used for a single negotiation, it also allows the application to keep state about the negotiation or context in the object.

(Callbacks won’t really work here because we don’t have a way to return a value from a callback, except by passing a Future in, which is at least weird.)

All snowflakes are unique, hence why they're called snowflakes and hence why the following was stated in my previous comment:
> some unique identifier stuff to ensure it's one-of-a-kind
All discord objects generate from the same snowflake system, there's not one special one for channels, another special one for users, etc. That would just be unnecessary complexity when the snowflakes by design are already ensured they're unique.

Your idea of doing a check for one object type followed by another, and just lazily adding all IDs in one table is a terrible data storage decision. All data should be kept distinct from each other in order to efficiently retrieve the information you require with the least amount of resources possible.

The sad story behind this is that indeed I'm observing problems when using `EMD2D` or `BEMD` on large or complex images. This seems to be related mainly to poor extrema detection and their handling. The algorithms used for detection (through erosion or checking surrounding) will return too many extrema for the RBF spline to handle leading to `Out of Memory` exception.

This was meant to be an easy extension which would hopefully be useful. Unfortunately EMD2D/BEMD are the extensions that disappoint (too often). Due to lack of time and shift in interest I'm unable to maintain/fix them. 

Since it might be useful to someone, I've decided to keep the code in the package, although warnings were added. Please feel free to propose modifications.

One of the possibilities, which I'm not sure if it will become real, is to add binding to [this](https://github.com/willdurand/EMD) C++ code. But this is a big *maybe* and please don't expect it soon.

I found that the reason for this is that **/user/{username}/saved.json** only returns partial data for the comment objects. Notable fields that are missing include "submission" and "permalink".  When we try to access any of these missing fields, praw will try to auto-load the comment. I had to work around this by going a step further from ``coerce_saved_comment`` and implementing a custom loader for the new **SavedComment** type.

That fix doesn't look right. It's just re-rolling the dice. A different RSA key will hit the problem with the new input. Rather, the bug is in this function and `int_to_bytes`:
https://github.com/nabla-c0d3/tls_parser/blob/master/tls_parser/handshake_protocol.py#L91

When you serialize your RSA ciphertext, it must have the same length as the modulus, zero-padded as necessary. (A production RSA implementation would get that padding for free by way of using fixed-width integers throughout, rather than explicitly zero-padding.)

PS: As for the "Not sure what this is?" comment in that file, that's the length of the ciphertext, which that file is currently incorrectly hardcoding at 256 bytes or 2048 bits. That would give the wrong answer for, say, a 3072-bit RSA key. Instead, you need to take the length and encode it as a big-endian 16-bit number. See [RFC 5246, section 4.7](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5246#section-4.7):

>    In public key encryption, a public key algorithm is used to encrypt
>   data in such a way that it can be decrypted only with the matching
>   private key.  A public-key-encrypted element is encoded as an opaque
>   vector <0..2^16-1>, where the length is specified by the encryption
>   algorithm and key.

(That <0..2^16-1> means there is a 16-bit length prefix.)

It's the same little test code I use for all my SBCs. The interesting thing is the SPI version of SSD1306 works as expected with the Duo. I've only seen this with the SSD1331 on the Duo and CHIP (it works fine on ODROID C1 and Pi 3 powered off the SBC). This is leading me to believe it's less to do with the SPI although I would not rule that out. Technically with the Duo SPI 0.0 is dedicated to flash and only 1.0 and 1.1 is available. I tried to configure 1.1, but it causes the Duo to act wried (the heartbeat LED stops flashing). I've used spidev to do a MO <-> MI loop back test and it works as expected.

I have 100nF and 10nF capacitors, but I've not seen anyone wire one of these in and I'm a little leery to be the first one :) I've never had to add caps or resistors to these little displays as they have built in voltage generator. You can see where I have the SSD1331 and ST7735 working with the ODROID C1+ here https://forum.odroid.com/viewtopic.php?f=116&t=26674

```
import time
import nanopi.duo
from OPi import GPIO
from luma.core.interface.serial import spi
from luma.core.render import canvas
from luma.oled.device import ssd1331

GPIO.setmode(nanopi.duo.BOARD)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
serial = spi(port=1, device=0, gpio=GPIO, gpio_DC=5, gpio_RST=3)
device = ssd1331(serial)

with canvas(device) as draw:
    draw.rectangle(device.bounding_box, outline="white")
    draw.text((3, 3), "Hello", fill="white")

time.sleep(10)
```
Tests require pytest and pytest-runner: https://github.com/sbraz/pymediainfo/blob/a3f6f986269f718758957cdbbde3aec7a936ee80/setup.py#L15

pdtContext is actually an indication of the components included in the original phrase, so having a flag in there to specify whether or not the parsed phrase included the am/pm meridian would allow developers to more easily implement custom handling. For instance, if the safe times concept was not implemented directly in parsedatetime, you could at least implement some code to check the context and adjust the parsed time based on your application's preferences.

So, when the date "1:12 pm" is parsed you would get a pdtContext specifying that the hour, minute, and meridian were set whereas parsing "1:12" would give you a pdtContext specifying that only the hour and minute were set. An application could check for that case and adjust the parsed time as needed.

> I guess the question is: Who is responsible for taking care of that bit of accounting? Does the ODBC spec say anything specific about Unicode encoding, especially with regard to UCS-2 (where every supported character is represented by exactly 2 bytes) versus UTF-16 (where some characters are represented by more than 2 bytes)?

The ODBC spec only mentions that the "column size" parameter is the "size in characters", and gives the example that "the column size of a single-byte character column defined as CHAR(10) is 10". Thus, it seems this is left to the DBMS to decide; in the case of SQL Server, an nvarchar(n) column in SQL Server can store exactly n UCS-2 characters:

https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/t-sql/data-types/nchar-and-nvarchar-transact-sql

Thus, a UTF-16 character which is actually a surrogate pair takes 2 "characters" of storage in an nvarchar(n) column, much like MBCS characters will count towards multiple "characters" of a varchar(n).

I do not think this issue is due to unixODBC, since it just passes the column size parameter from the application to the driver; it is related to how pyodbc is calculating it.

This could be a remnant of Python2. If an output converter function is in play then pyodbc always receives the column value as bytes and passes them to the function for further manipulation. In Python2, `str` was the "sequence of bytes" data type.

yeah, this is rough. 

i think i'm gonna have to just create these as their own pipelines. 

featureunion appears to be making all this difficult. so let's avoid it. 

let's assume two subpredictors. 

1. iterate through our training dataset, splitting out 20% of the data to train our subpredictors. 
1. import auto_ml, and invoke it (twice) to create trained pipelines. we can, of course, pass in X_test and y_test (or any part of the 80% of our training set not used for the subpredictors) to validate and score these pipelines returned to us from auto_ml
1. if we want to train up weak classifiers on our final y data, we'd use this 20% split for that purpose too, though that's likely sometime in the future. 
1. once we have our two trained pipelines for our subpredictors, we can pass them into a new module that goes into our main pipeline. this module, on __init__, will take in as many pipelines as we have subpredictors. it does nothing on fit (we're only gonna fit these models once at the very start, to avoid retraining them again and again and again). and on transform (or predict), it will return the original X, plus two new attributes, one each for both of our subpredictors. 
1. this new module will have to go very early in the pipeline, probably before anything else (possibly after user_defined_func if we pass that through to the subpredictor pipelines, but that seems like a lot of repeated computations). orrr, we could possibly run user_defined_func just once outside of any of the pipelines, before we do anything else. then in auto_ml.predict, we'll have to manually call that before passing data into the trained_pipeline. but that seems reasonable. 
1. when we call transform on this new module, it will simply run the raw input data through these two subpredictors, and append their output as attributes onto the raw X objects. 
1. Then the rest of the pipeline continues on as normal. 

no need for featureunions in this setup.

Quick brain dump:

- At the docutils level, Sphinx is simply never asking it to `add_role` the traditional Sphinx roles like `doc`, `ref`, etc etc.
- However it _is_ calling `add_role` for things like `manpage`, `program`, and even `any` and `pep`.
- Seems like the start of the divergence is around `Sphinx.setup_extension`, or rather what it calls, which is `sphinx.roles.setup()`.
- The actual content of `sphinx.roles.setup` is the same in both Sphinx 1.5 and 1.6, but it seems like the contents of the data it iterates over - `specific_docroles` - is different. (Some values within that structure, like `any` and `pep`, are [as noted above] still present in 1.6, so it's not as simple as the entire thing being moved elsewhere...)
- Still digging into what that structure is & why it's apparently different now.

#!/usr/bin/env python

#
###############################################################################
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,
                        unicode_literals)

import argparse
import datetime

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds
import backtrader.utils.flushfile


class St(bt.Strategy):
    def next(self):
        print(','.join(
            [self.data.datetime.datetime().isoformat(),
             str(self.data.open[0]),
             str(self.data.high[0]),
             str(self.data.low[0]),
             str(self.data.close[0])]))


def runstrat():
    args = parse_args()

    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()

    data = btfeeds.GenericCSVData(dataname=args.data,
                                  timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Ticks,
                                  dtformat='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f')

    cerebro.resampledata(data,
                         timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Minutes,
                         compression=1,
                         bar2edge=True,
                         adjbartime=True,
                         rightedge=False)

    cerebro.addstrategy(St)
    cerebro.run()

    if args.plot:
        cerebro.plot()


def parse_args():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        formatter_class=argparse.ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter,
        description='Sample for aligning with trade ')

    parser.add_argument('--data', required=True,
                        help='Data to be read in')

    parser.add_argument('--plot', required=False,
                        default=False, action='store_true', help='Do plot')

    return parser.parse_args()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    runstrat()
```

Since [v0.3.0](https://github.com/vxgmichel/aiostream/releases/tag/v0.3.0), it's possible to implement `as_completed` using `map`:

```python
from aiostream import stream, operator

@operator
async def as_completed(*coroutines, task_limit=None):
    async def corofn(coro):
        return await coro
    xs = stream.create.raw(coroutines)
    return stream.map.raw(xs, corofn, ordered=False, task_limit=task_limit)
```

But I think it's even better to let map handle the creation of the tasks, so it can be done in a lazy way:
 
```python
xs = stream.iterate(args_gen)
ys = xs | pipe.map(corofn, ordered=True, task_limit=10)
```

So after looking into this a bit, this appears to be the correct behavior of the error report mode "report" which SpiceyPy sets in the __init__.py for the package. For more info on what modes exist please check out: "http://naif.jpl.nasa.gov/pub/naif/toolkit_docs/C/cspice/erract_c.html". 

Now the next part gets a bit philosophical, if the user sets the mode "ignore" no spice errors are reported and subsequent calls using the kernel pool do function. But as this stops any errors being reported it will be up to the user to determine is something bad happened. The alternative mode is "return" which does still report errors however calls will fail silently until `SpiceyPy.reset()` is called. 

I think that the ideal solution would still report errors, but without forcing the user to call reset. Or maybe that is fine?

> However I would not rush into a definitive conclusion. This is the kind of topic that requires careful analysis and design.

Definitely. Dethroning a well entrenched industry tool is not a light task.

> What is your estimate regarding the acquisition rate? 

Assume 1 Mb/s as a minimum. It's the max data rate for existing CAN buses. J1939 is still at 250 kB/s. 

Eventually I'd like to workup to using the MPC5748G as a CAN<->Ethernet gateway and it supports up to 8 FlexCAN with optional CAN FD support.

Small SBCs these days are as fast as the machines we had a decade ago doing CANape work. The [CANcaseXL Loggers](https://vector.com/portal/medien/cmc/manuals/CANcaseXL_Manual_EN.pdf) are tiny embedded devices writing to SD cards (and haven't been upgraded that I've seen in a while). I don't think we should have a problem keeping up as long as the disk can handle the write speed.

**uvloop** is an async I/O event loop and has some pretty decent benchmarks: https://magic.io/blog/uvloop-blazing-fast-python-networking/ Granted this is serving message requests but @ 1 KiB message sizes it's serving 102MiB/s, and 100 KiB message sizes 2.3GiB/s. As long as we're not doing any heavy filtering or data processing I think a Pi should be able to handle the data rates. 

A machine with at least 2 cores should have no problem keeping up with reading packets and flushing them to disk.

It seems the system's `locale` only supports ascii characters (e.g. empty `LANG`, `LANG=C`, `LANG=POSIX`) when there is a unicode character found in `/etc/apt/sources.list`. I believe official Ubuntu docker images are examples of systems where locale is configured to only support ascii by default.

Setting `LANG=C.UTF-8` in the environment should resolve it.

However, to support comments with unicode characters in `/etc/apt/sources.list`, I think we could easily fix it here by using the [`io` module](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/io.html)'s `open` like:

```python
with io.open('/etc/apt/sources.list', encoding='utf-8') as f:
```

Then, encode the replaced string before writing it to the new file.

Related to #67, but different in that a fix should not require ignoring or replacing non ascii characters before trying to write to the console.

I'll note that this would not be an issue in Python 3.7 where [PEP 538](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0538/) is now implemented.

I'm not keen on this adding more fields to the Tx datastructure.  That is intended to be a simple representation of a Tx, and keeping it minimal in code and data size is important for initial sync.  Moreover this feature is only a need of a single chain ElectrumX supports but all chains are paying the cost.

Since this functionality is about mempools only, I would like this to be isolated to mempool code that processes Txs.  Mempool processing is fast on most chains as mempools are small, so that seems a better place for the burden.

If tx_hash -> (txin_pairs, txout_pairs, tx_fee, tx_size) is the best data structure (I'm not sure; I've not thought about it much), I think we should make that a named tuple.

It seems you're recomputing the histogram every time there is no mempool processing to do.  This might be a bit frequent and CPU intensive, particularly with 150k txs in the mempool.  What do you think of every 10 minutes and every new block?  I'm not sure how you are implementing this on the client side - is it queried every time a user tries to construct a tx to send, or is it just regularly polled?

The new API should only be provided for 1.2 clients (see how other RPC methods are restricted by client protocol version).

Python uses a hierarchical logging system. So, if you create a bunch of loggers like:
1. electrumx.server.SomeClass
2. electrumx.server.SomeOtherClass
3. electrumx.lib.AnotherClass

You can control logging at the root package level, at sub packages or at the class level:

1. `logging.getLogger('electrumx').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)` will set logging for everything under `electrumx.
1. `logging.getLogger('electrumx.server').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)` will set it for everything under `electrumx.server`.
1. `logging.getLogger('electrumx.server.BlockchainProcessor').setLevel(logging.DEBUG)` will set it for everything under `electrumx.server.BlockchainProcessor`.

The `__name__` is just the dot delimited module path. So, `logging.getLogger(__name__).getChild(self.__class__.__name__)` translates to something like `logging.getLogger('electrumx.server').getChild('BlockchainProcessor')`.

Also, see: https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.Logger.getChild

I'm going to vote against the proposed implementation of this one. Something like this would be better implemented as a logic adapter as ChatterBot moves towards a muti-adapter structure in the future. As a logic adapter the setup would need to check if the input value contained a time-centric question and then return the current time as a response statement.

The reason for this design choice is to ensure greater flexibility in the future by preferring analysis over regex. A logic adapter would be modular and easily maintained, while a training method such as the one proposed leaves the database in a state in which it will always require statements to be checked for this dynamic format.

**The most ideal implementation** would be a logic adapter that internally trains itself based on a set of similar questions such as "What time is it?", "Do you know the time?", etc. As well as false values such as "Do you have a dog?", "Is it time to go?", etc. The adapter should then recognize when an input statement contains a valid question and return the current time accordingly. This would provide the greatest flexibility, likely accomplish language independence, and still accomplish the desired result as proposed initially.

```python
# monkeypatch https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0476/

    import ssl

```

# the oauth2 flow stays the same as in df2gspread/utils.py:get_credentials()

I thought the app was properly field tested and safe, but it's not. By the way, you may want to read up on another issue in Simon Willison's (co-founder of Django) blog post about this type of rate limiting:
http://blog.simonwillison.net/post/57956846132/ratelimitcache

The other issue is: Imagine a rate limit of 5,000/h. That's not uncommon, e.g. for an API. What about if a user makes 5,000 requests just a minute before the cache key expires and none before? Right after that, he can make another 5,000 requests. Which results in almost 10,000 requests/h. Thus, the rate limit was breached.

        if self.max_value:
            self.widget_kwargs.setdefault(
                'samples', max(10, self.max_value / 100))
```

The AdaptiveETA class is written to take the last 10 samples by default.  However due to the snip above if max_value > 1000 it will use the last max_value/100 samples.  I think this makes sense, in theory the more numbers in max_value the more frequent the bar would be updated.  Take the following two examples, they both result in "normal" adaptiveETA behavior.  

```python
interval = 1000

print('Testing working code with small interval. Notice the ETA after', interval // 2)
pbr = progressbar.ProgressBar(maxval=interval)

# Do some "faster" operations
for i in range(0, interval // 2, interval // 100):
    time.sleep(0.25)
    pbr.update(i)
# Perform some "slower" operations
for i in range(interval // 2, interval, interval // 100):
    time.sleep(0.75)
    pbr.update(i)

pbr.finish()


interval = 10000

print('Testing code with 10x as frequent updates. Notice the ETA after', interval // 2)
pbr = progressbar.ProgressBar(maxval=interval)

# Do some "faster" operations
for i in range(0, interval // 2, interval // 1000):
    time.sleep(0.25 / 10)
    pbr.update(i)
# Perform some "slower" operations
for i in range(interval // 2, interval, interval // 1000):
    time.sleep(0.75 / 10)
    pbr.update(i)

pbr.finish()
```
However I could see having very large max_value's and a small amount of  updates, for example some file transfer algroithm that is transferring 1MB file in 1KB chunks.  With the existing code it would try to use 10,000 updates in the ETA algorithm which would result in the same behavior you saw.  You could just specify widgets_kwargs?  

```python
pbr = progressbar.ProgressBar(maxval=interval, widget_kwargs={'samples':10})
```

I'm not sure this is the best method, the other method would be to intelligently look at the average update difference to determine about how many updates it will take to fill the whole progressbar and then use that number to calculate how many samples to use for the adaptiveETA

Generally, trusted certificates should be handled outside exchangelib, and even outside Python. exchangelib uses `requests`, which again uses OpenSSL (I believe), so you should add the new certificate to the system-wide certificate store so OpenSSL can use it.

However, it's possible to customize the transport adapter class that exchangelib uses, so customize e.g. certificate validation. See the bottom of the 'setup' section: https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib#setup-and-connecting and the corresponding documentation in http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/api/#requests.adapters.BaseAdapter

Another possibility is to use the `REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE` environment variable. See http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification You may also be able to handle this with the `certifi` package, see the section on CA certificates: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ca-certificates

@tkoroscha 

I have never used virtual channels. I started with FHEM + HM-CFG-LAN, and then switched to Home-Assistant + Homegear. So I never used some of the more sophisitcated features the CCU provides.
I assume in your case you have manually activated the virtual channels. But I guess they won't be needed for usage with Home Assistant. They seem to provide helping functionality for better automations. But that's something you would do within Home Assistant, since that's where you control the devices from. Hence I think the virtual channels are not needed.

The genreal question would be: what would you expect Home Assistant to be able to do? The regular dimmers I have seen so far just have the `LEVEL` parameter, which is used to control the brightness in %. Those don't have colors though. I have Philips Hue Lamps, and to control their color Home Assistant does pass RGB values. Something similar would be needed for the LED controller. But according to the paramsets there's nothing where I could push `#aabbcc` or `128,128,128`. You say the dual white mode uses the second channel for the color. So I imagine setting `LEVEL` would use the _Farbkreis_, where 0.01 would be red, raising the number cycles through the colors, and at 0.99 you're at (almost) red again. In that case we'd need to convert RGB to such a scale.
If using the independent mode it would be more complicated I guess.

Maybe you could go through some manual steps and report how the device reacts.

```python
from xmlrpc.client import ServerProxy
p = ServerProxy("http://192.168.1.23:2001") # Whatever IP you have, 2001 for wireless, 2010 for IP devices
# Get the current state of channel 1. What's the light like with those values?
print(p.getValue("OEQ0611258:1", "LEVEL"))
print(p.getValue("OEQ0611258:1", "LEVEL_REAL"))
print(p.getValue("OEQ0611258:1", "OLD_LEVEL"))
# How does the light change when you execute these commands?
p.setValue("OEQ0611258:1", "LEVEL", 0.5)
p.setValue("OEQ0611258:1", "LEVEL_REAL", 0.5)
# Has the OLD_LEVEL changed by setting the values above? If so, does it match LEVEL or LEVEL_REAL?
print(p.getValue("OEQ0611258:1", "OLD_LEVEL"))
```

You can do the same for channel 2 and report what is happening.
We also need some way to figure out to which mode the device is configured. Keep an eye on the `DIRECTION` parameter when experimenting with the code above. Or play around with `p.getParamsetDescription("OEQ0611258:0", "MASTER")` or `p.getParamset("OEQ0611258:0", "MASTER")` (also try the other channels).

Lights in general usually are in [actors.py](https://github.com/danielperna84/pyhomematic/blob/master/pyhomematic/devicetypes/actors.py). The first thing you could try would be to add `"HM-LC-DW-WM": Dimmer` to the dictionary at the bottom. That would even work with Home Assistant und should be able to at least control the brightness on channel 1. If you add a case for the LED-controller [here](https://github.com/danielperna84/pyhomematic/blob/49c3498944ca5e4309ccc8dc8987611ff57e7ffe/pyhomematic/devicetypes/actors.py#L88) you could even add the 2. channel like it's done with _Dim2L_.
To also allow changing the color with a separate function (one that would accept rgb like Home Assistant would push), we would need a sub-class of the Dimmer with some sort of `set_color` method. That custom class would then need to be added to [homematic/__init__.py](https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/blob/f56b3d8e9ce4a63521e36215f22c078a0e55c14c/homeassistant/components/homematic/__init__.py#L63) in the Home Assistant code. And in [light/homematic.py](https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/blob/dev/homeassistant/components/light/homematic.py) you would need to add the functionality to change the color, which until now isn't possible with any HomeMatic device I think.

I have to admit that I didn't check the `get_or_set` implementation for reference when implementing that and assumed that `None` is a valid value to be put as default, simply because it's allowed with the "raw" `get`/`set` functions.

```python
>>> cache.set('aaa', None)
True
>>> sent = object()
>>> cache.get('aaa', default=sent)
>>> cache.get('aab', default=sent)
<object object at 0x7fef03eab560>
```

However, as the function name suggests that it just implements a locked copy of the Django `get_or_set` method, I agree that it's a better idea to copy that behavior and just take `None` as marker for non-existent keys.

As a quick workaround (which certainly doesn't speed things up), the resolution can be increased
with `scipy.ndimage.zoom`. This has the added advantage of isosurfaces working in the same fashion with no additional work. 

<details>
<summary>Example Code</summary>

```
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import ipyvolume as ipv
from scipy.ndimage import zoom
import numpy as np
%matplotlib inline

# Double the resolution n times
def double_n(arr, n):
    for i in range(n):
        # Note: a value > 2 can be given,
        # but for some reason the center
        # grid cells become larger than
        # those on the edges, which is
        # not what I want here.
        arr = zoom(arr, 2, order=0)
    return arr

# Due to a bug in IPyVolume (#117, fixed but not released),
# there are two versions: One with the correct scale
# and one with isosurfaces.

def volshow_with_isoplanes_and_zoom(x_list, y_list, z_list, f, zoom_factor):
    # zoom_factor is the number of times the grid is doubled
    # (actually a zoom by 2 ** zoom_factor)
    
    # Assume constant spacing in each dimension
    dx = np.mean(np.diff(x_list))
    dy = np.mean(np.diff(y_list))
    dz = np.mean(np.diff(z_list))
    
    # zoom
    x_grid, y_grid, z_grid = np.meshgrid(x_list, y_list, z_list, indexing='ij')
    new_f = double_n(f, zoom_factor)
    
    # data extent
    extent = (
        (min(x_list)-dx/2, max(x_list)+dx/2),
        (min(y_list)-dy/2, max(y_list)+dy/2),
        (min(z_list)-dz/2, max(z_list)+dz/2)
    )
    
    ipv.figure()
    
    # isosurfaces
    ipv.plot_isosurface(new_f)
    
    # volshow
    ipv.volshow(new_f.T)
    
    ipv.show()

def volshow_zoom_correct_scale(x_list, y_list, z_list, f, zoom_factor):
    # zoom_factor is the number of times the grid is doubled
    # (actually a zoom by 2 ** zoom_factor)
    
    # Assume constant spacing in each dimension
    dx = np.mean(np.diff(x_list))
    dy = np.mean(np.diff(y_list))
    dz = np.mean(np.diff(z_list))
    
    # zoom
    x_grid, y_grid, z_grid = np.meshgrid(x_list, y_list, z_list, indexing='ij')
    new_f = double_n(f, zoom_factor)
    
    # data extent
    extent = (
        (min(x_list)-dx/2, max(x_list)+dx/2),
        (min(y_list)-dy/2, max(y_list)+dy/2),
        (min(z_list)-dz/2, max(z_list)+dz/2)
    )
    
    ipv.figure()
    
    # volshow
    ipv.volshow(new_f.T)
    
    # figure extent
    ipv.xlim(min(x_list)-dx/2, max(x_list)+dx/2)
    ipv.ylim(min(y_list)-dy/2, max(y_list)+dy/2)
    ipv.zlim(min(z_list)-dz/2, max(z_list)+dz/2)
    
    ipv.show()

x_list = range(10, 18, 2)
y_list = range(4, 10, 2)
z_list = range(20, 30, 2)
x_grid, y_grid, z_grid = np.meshgrid(x_list, y_list, z_list, indexing='ij')
f = x_grid + y_grid - z_grid

volshow_zoom_correct_scale(x_list, y_list, z_list, f, zoom_factor=5)
volshow_with_isoplanes_and_zoom(x_list, y_list, z_list, f, zoom_factor=5)
```

</details>

one limitation of the current rate limiter that i know of is that pending requests are not checked before a request is made. if 25 requests are made at the same time with a limit of 20 request, 5 should currently get a 429. 

That comment is specifically talking about when the rate limit resets. Assume you have the limit of 10 calls/min. If you make a 2 requests right as the rate limit is about to reset, request A and request B. Response A is sent back with the header saying 10 requests have been made (so the next call will be limited). Response B is sent back with a header saying that 1 request has been made (the rate limit has reset). If response B is processed before response A, then what will happen is that the count will be set to 1, and then set to 10, leading all subsequent calls to be rate limited for a minute. 

I believe that 2nd case is not a very likely case, but i believe it is possible, which is why that comment exists.

regarding the issue writing to the tuple, that seems like a pretty clear bug and should be easy to solve.

Can you split this into 2 separate issues? one for the clear bug writing things that are not writable, and one for the fact that pending requests are not tracked?

@Diaoul: There's a lot to like about your approach, and it seems to make sense. I like that the parent seems to be explicit in the child ViewSet.

I like the approach, I think it's good work. I do see some deficiencies, but this may be a useful starting point.

There should be a parallel to `lookup_url_kwarg`, which is separate from the `parent_lookup_field`. This would allow me to use `client_id` as the url kwarg, instead of just `client`.

I also wonder if there is a reasonable place to check that the router URL config is properly matching the parent relationship as required by the view. At first glance, it seems to me that only the router would be able to check that, so it would have to be a special cased check when the ViewSet is determined to have used the `NestedViewSetMixin`.

-----------

And the one thing that I see that may kill this `get_nested_filter_dict` approach is that it doesn't take into account any of the custom queryset filtering that may have been implemented in the parent `ViewSet`. If that's a needed feature, that could really put a kink in things.

The most obvious solution would be to use the parent's `get_object` method (which uses `get_queryset`) to get the instance, and then follow a relationship on that object. That would require a separate query for each ViewSet, but would enforce all the parent's queryset constraints, as well as any permissions and filtering, on the parent as well, which I'm pretty certain that this would not be able to accomplish. Whether that's actually a good idea, or if it needs to be considered at the permission level is a good question.

I'm attempting to implement this repo into my testing package, and hit an interesting issue between the `nested_dict` implementation and the `UpdateCache` class.

If the cache has already been populated, and you then add/update a run:
```
runs = tr.runs()
run = tr.run()
run.name = "Foo"
run.project = tr.project(1)
tr.add(run)
```
The UpdateCache updating works as expected. 

If you do the same thing without first populating the cache:
```
# runs = tr.runs()
run = tr.run()
run.name = "Foo"
run.project = tr.project(1)
tr.add(run)
```
UpdateCache throws an exception:
`AttributeError: 'collections.defaultdict' object has no attribute 'append'`

The problem lies with the way `nested_dict` is building out the initial cache structure. When you examine the cache structure when `tr.runs()` is run first, it looks like so:
```
(Pdb) print(self.cache)
defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x105262510>, {
    15: defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x105262510>, {
        'value': [
            {'untested_count': 3, 'suite_id': 18, ...
```
Note that the deepest container (for `value`) is a list of dicts.

If you examine the cache when `tr.runs()` has not been run first, and an add/update is about to happen, it looks like so:
```
(Pdb) pp(self.cache)
defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x10594d510>, {
    15: defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x10594d510>, {
        'value': defaultdict(<function <lambda> at 0x10594d510>, {})})})
```
Where `value`'s container is now a defaultdict.

Unfortunately, `SmartQueryMixin.smart_query` is not intended to work with aggregation functions like `COUNT`, `SUM` etc. And, for now, I don't see a way how to implement such feature. But thanks for idea!

====

Generally speaking, filtering by aggregated function is quite complex task in SQL.
For your task, SQL should look like 
```sql
SELECT * FROM post 
LEFT JOIN comment ON post.id = comment.post_id 
GROUP BY post.id 
HAVING count(comment.id) < 10
```

Corresponding SQLAlchemy code will be
```python
Post.query.outerjoin(Post.comments).group_by(Post.id).having(func.count(Comment.id) < 10).all()
```
You can test above code on [examples/all_features.py](https://github.com/absent1706/sqlalchemy-mixins/blob/master/examples/all_features.py#L67)

====

In real project, I prefer to have a computed `comments_count` field in `post` table. This field is updated automatically due to [this awesome plugin](https://sqlalchemy-utils.readthedocs.io/en/latest/aggregates.html#simple-aggregates). It simplifies query building and reduces database load.

So I have 
```python
class Post
   # ...
   @aggregated('comments', sa.Column(sa.Integer))
   def comment_count(self):
      return sa.func.count('1')
```

And just write
```python
Post.where(comments_count__lt=10).all()
```

I strongly recommend you such approach

Short answer is no, but we absolutely need this, particularly to represent complex data types which don't work within the constraints of form-data.

Couple of things we need to accomplish this is:
- Adjustment of how the encoding is set for the Document by the schema generator:
https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/e3f8d06bafd37780d6010b0a963029a3448d5e54/rest_framework/schemas.py#L244-L263

  If the parser/renderer classes support form data (default), the encoding on the Document should be set to x-www-form. If not (and presuming JSON), then the encoding should be set to application/json

- CoreAPI currently supports 3 types of parameter locations: `query`, `path` and `form`. For `application/json` types we'd want a location something like `body` with a schema (similarly to OpenAPI).

from modeltranslation.manager import MultilingualManager, MultilingualQuerysetManager
from modeltranslation.translator import has_custom_queryset

def patch_manager_class(manager):
    if isinstance(manager, MultilingualManager):
        return
    if manager.__class__ is Manager:
        manager.__class__ = MultilingualManager
    else:
        class NewMultilingualManager(MultilingualManager, manager.__class__,
                                     MultilingualQuerysetManager):
            use_for_related_fields = getattr(
                manager.__class__, "use_for_related_fields", not has_custom_queryset(manager))

                    False,  # as_manager
                    '%s.%s' % (self._old_module, self._old_class),  # manager_class
                    None,  # qs_class
                    self._constructor_args[0],  # args
                    self._constructor_args[1],  # kwargs

I extracted code above directly from modeltranslation/translator.py and used it to reapply the modeltranslation manager patch to original managers from the abstract class.

Anyway debugging modeltranslation/translator.py code
```
    managers = (model._meta.local_managers if NEW_MANAGER_API else
                (getattr(model, x[1]) for x in
                 model._meta.concrete_managers + model._meta.abstract_managers))
    for current_manager in managers:
        prev_class = current_manager.__class__
        patch_manager_class(current_manager)
        if model._default_manager.__class__ is prev_class:
            # Normally model._default_manager is a reference to one of model's managers
            # (and would be patched by the way).
            # However, in some rare situations (mostly proxy models)
            # model._default_manager is not the same instance as one of managers, but it
            # share the same class.
            model._default_manager.__class__ = current_manager.__class__
    patch_manager_class(model._base_manager)
    if hasattr(model._meta, "_expire_cache"):
        model._meta._expire_cache()
```

I noticed:
1) model._meta.local_managers is always empty
2) that changing it to model._meta.managers (it's holding right managers list instead) it worked
3) finally model._meta._expire_cache() destroy patch just installed by 2).

Moving away code related to point 3) (before ```managers = (model._meta.local_managers if NEW_MANAGER_API...``` for example) apparently fixed the issue in the original code but I am not sure if this would work for all cases.

Our initial grief were the slow and incomplete ufuncs and we'd love to see a faster version integrated into numpy as well. I agree, our main focus was getting a reasonably fast version right away, only aiming for a numpy integration in the very long run. We had started an [issue](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/5922) on the numpy bugtracker once discussing the slow ufuncs, though it didn't look like there would be an easy fix for it.

In terms of speed, what we see by benchmarking our numba and weave code, it runs about 30x faster than numpy ufuncs. Using `np.bincount` or plain indexing, things get better for numpy, and as you said, the hand crafted code is only 2-4x faster for some functions. Still, looking at things like grouped cumsum, required to do some sorting with plain numpy, you see a 100x speedup.

About external dependencies, I agree, the less the better. I don't want to force anyone to use numba, just for doing some indexed operations. At the same time there are applications (having such one on my own), where you just want to run things as fast as possible and don't mind installing another dependency, or you use those dependencies anyways already. Our approach to that was having different implementations for different tastes / requirements, sharing the same interface. I guess that might also be a reasonable approach for a possible combined project.

So, I agree, goals and emphasis are a bit different, but imho still not that far off, that a combined package would seem unrealistic. In terms of generality and clean interface, full acc. It also wouldn't need to happen right away. I guess adding a wrapper around numpy-groupies, providing an numpy-indexed-like interface might make sense, and mb also the other way round. Mb providing a common interface at some point and agreeing on how an interface for implementations with different dependencies might look like. Anyways, up to you. Mb you want to give it another thought!

Kind regards,

Michael

This is dependent on your Spark setup.  For instance, in our case, we run Spark on top of Hadoop/YARN, so YARN is responsible for allocating containers to run the Spark executors (which in turn run the TensorFlow nodes).  Unfortunately, YARN currently does not have GPUs as a schedulable resource.  Instead, YARN schedules generally on CPU and Memory, so in our case, we use Memory as a proxy for GPU.

So, in your example, if we assume that your nodes are 64GB nodes with 4 GPUs each, then we'd schedule a GPU by requesting 16GB of memory.  And, if this proxy is consistently used, then a node with all four GPUs in use (i.e. 64GB memory) would not be scheduled for any new executors/containers by YARN.

The reasoning behind this logic is that I brought in lapack as a new dependency of MXNet. But that dependency is only needed for very specific applications/operators. We (Eric & me) didn't want to have all people in the world that use MXNet already and just trying to update it to a new version to have suddenly their installation failing because they are missing lapack (which they don't need at all). This is the case for Ubuntu 14.04 (as there installing openblas will not install lapack automatically) and potentially other platforms. We rather decided to give a runtime-error for people that use the new functionality.   
So this is all about making sure that the user community goes through minimal hassle. In the long run, we may phase out that logic, but not now.

As seen above for float 16, the output that is close to 0.999 rounds to 1.0. Our default margin for SVMOutput layer is 1.0

In L1_SVM function of the operator we compare margin > src (where src is the outputs) 

For float32 this resolves to 1.0 > 0.99532
For float16 this resolves to 1.0 > 1.0

Thus showing wrong results in grad (Type casted from bool to float) which is 1.0 and 0.0 thus resulting in the error

To fix this the input being provided for test, should not be normally distributed, as values closer to the margin, will break the condition due to rounding at float16 level.

One solution is to provide the test with a uniform distribution of inputs , that will never get close to the margin.

>
> @adriancole <https://github.com/adriancole> I suppose that 'sa'
> annotation is just a common place where we can store server address, if one
> service uses DNS address to access other service, then we should be able to
> place this DNS in 'sa', don't they?
>
"sa" is special as the value is stored in the Endpoint "binary annotation"
(unlike normal tags where the value is a string). This is defined in thrift
and only includes a resolved ipv4 or ipv6 ip or port (if present). Often, a
client interceptor or callback can show the actual IP address used for the
connection. If not, sometimes people resolve DNS, but that could be tricky.
Often it is better to add the tag/binary annotation "http.host" separately
if you don't know the actual IP address, but have some host info.

So, "sa" should be a binary annotation with key = "sa", type=bool, value=[1]
endpoint.serviceName=name_of_remote_service
endpoint.(ipv4|ipv6)=exact_ip_used or absent if not known
endpoint.port=exact_port_used or absent if not known

https://github.com/openzipkin/zipkin-api/blob/master/thrift/zipkinCore.thrift

In the future, this will be simplified to span.remoteService with exactly
the same data

I haven't looked at the NTFS change journal API, but watchman does have a reasonably modular way to plug in an alternative watcher implementation (watcher/win32.c is currently the only windows implementation); it might be interesting to try building a change journal watcher to contrast/compare. It does sound a bit more painful to configure and deploy, so it probably would not be the default mode of operation. In addition, the ReadDirectoryChangeW API should work with more filesystems.
I don't think we're actively going to try the change journal ourselves, but I'm definitely open to reviewing and evaluating that contribution :-)

I made a pull request for the `translate` function. It ignores the `plone.api` `get_current_language` function.

Note that the negotiated language can also [differ per translation domain](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.i18n/blob/4.2.0/src/zope/i18n/translationdomain.py#L78-L83), which our `get_current_language` function does not account for. So for example you may run into things like this, even without combined language codes:

- The visitor prefers Dutch, then German, then English, so `get_current_language` returns Dutch.
- Currently our `translate` method would then pass Dutch as the *only* target language.
- But the domain may have only German translations.
- So we see no Dutch translations, and fall back to English, instead of trying German.

So we can keep `get_current_language`, and it will usually work, but it will not give us the correct answer in all cases. It could be fixable by passing the Message and/or the domain, but that seems weird. When the user does that, she obviously wants to translate, so she should use `api.portal.translate` instead. We can keep the function, but I don't think I will be using it.

@NicoHood You need git-lfs to use the git-stored model, under `data/lm`. If you downloaded the deepspeech-models tarball, you can just use the lm.binary and trie from your extraction.

Regarding your warning, that's because we document conservative optimizations. You have to weight the level of optimizations that you pass to TensorFlow with what users might have.

So since you passed nothing, except `--copt=-O3` then nothing is available. TensorFlow r1.6 (upcoming) defaulted to up-to AVX: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blame/26ae3287a12c71fccaec9ea74f55b6a51a3d33c6/RELEASE.md#L5

Our TaskCluster-builds are also upto AVX, by building everything with:
```
--copt=-mtune=generic --copt=-march=x86-64 --copt=-msse --copt=-msse2 --copt=-msse3 --copt=-msse4.1 --copt=-msse4.2 --copt=-mavx
```

Just be aware that if you enable some optimizations then TensorFlow (and thus DeepSpeech) won't be able to run on a hardware that does not have them. This is a limitation from TensorFlow :/.

Top10 is not a subset, it's all alerts but grouped first by count and then by duplicate count. It's meant to highlight alert sources that generate the most alerts so that you can address them if necessary. So in the above example, you can see that event "1221wsws" has generated 5 individual alerts for 5 different resources and 24 duplicates overall.

I find Top 10 useful when receiving a lot of syslog messages that turn out to be spam. I can quickly identify the worst offenders and modify the syslog parser to reject or reduce those alerts.

I just talked to @SylvainCorlay about the last point he proposed. I'm +1 for this proposal. `Observe` is like on trait change - which fires when the state of a value changes.  It just so happens that with things like evenful list and dict, the state is more complex and hence the change event can be more descriptive.  The should share `observe` instead of implementing custom decorators because the underlying concept is the same.

I should be clear though, I would refrain from using `observe` of any purpose other than listening to the state change of traits.  IOW, I still think it's appropriate to have other decorators, like `validate`.

**tl;dr**: `Parameter` should really be named `StrParameter` since it de/serializes strings; however, when using normal requirements this can be disregarded without unintended consequences, but misuse of `Parameter` within dynamic requirements will result in data treated as strings.

Through using dynamic requires, I found that anything passed as a `Parameter` was treated as a string (which is why I added PR #1614 ). 

Parameters take one of two paths within luigi execution: 1. dynamic requires 2. normal requires.

1. @davemt found that

> dynamic dependencies have `load_task` called on them before they are added to worker's task cache whereas regular `requires()` do not. This requires that a parameter value can be properly serialized/deserialized.

So since the `Parameter` is first serialized, it is cast as a string and then parsed (as a string). Thus no warning message.

2. However, during normal requires, there is no guaranteed serialization/deserialization. So you can (as I have historically) incorrectly pass any type of structure through as a `Parameter` (i.e. Lists, Tuples, etc.). 
So since `Parameter` assumes strings (as seen by its `serialize()` method), @erikbern suggested

> It might be good to do a warnings.warn in case serialize returns a non-string

I do not think there is anyway to get around the copy time from making the request. The best option you have is to make the part copies multithreaded to reduce time for the lambda function.

The good news is with the release of boto3 1.4.0, we have released a new managed ``copy()`` method that will do multipart copies autmatically and do it in parallel: https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/s3.html#copies. So you should see a speed up in using this as you no longer will just have to loop through these. So I would recommend using that method especially for multipart copies.

Resolving issue, as multipart copy sample is no longer needed with the managed ``copy()`` method.

I think the changes look good for now.  We can look at improvements in the future.

As for functional tests, I think of them as tests that operate at the API used by the customer (and on a technical level don't call out to AWS).  The whole idea is to exercise end to end functionality and catch problems such as events not hooked up properly, wrong assumptions about file system access, customizations we make that don't actually work with real world JSON models we use, etc.  The more that gets mocked/patched out, the less useful functional tests become for me.

You mentioned that are similar functional tests, but I think the differences between them are what make the difference for me.  For example, other functional tests only care that `upload_file` was added to a client.  They don't *which* upload_file method or *how* it was added, just that by the time you get a client, `upload_file` exists.
This gives us flexibility to alter the implementation as long as we make sure `upload_file` gets added.  I get that it's not as simple in this test because we're replacing, not adding a method, so you have to check that it was replaced with a *specific* method via `assertIs`.  That's why I feel here that an integ test would have been better.

FWIW, you could just create a resource that's cheap to create, like `CreateVPC`.

But either way, I think it's something we can look at later if this becomes an issue.

I was removing `~~~~` (the `+` is not in the file, that just belongs to the diff output). The reason for this is that all headings get assigned a target name, and RST doesn't like duplicate target names. You can see the errors here:

```
$ python setup.py check --restructuredtext --strict --metadata
running check
warning: check: Duplicate implicit target name: "bugfixes".

warning: check: Duplicate implicit target name: "bugfixes".

warning: check: Duplicate implicit target name: "features".

error: Please correct your package.
```

Normally, PyPI does not render the RST at all when it detects any errors, I can't explain why it does render just fine in this case.

There's no need to change HISTORY.txt as it doesn't get included in `long_description` in `setup.py`. If you want it to match visually, I can do that really quickly.

The current build docs test does not test that `long_description` in `setup.py` is valid RST, which the additional command does.

You can close this if you want, but as soon as something is released on PyPI that doesn't render the RST, remember `python setup.py check --restructuredtext --strict --metadata`, it's a useful command.

This comes from this line:
https://github.com/scoutapp/scout_apm_python/blob/bba1c0e441e5290ec5109e5dd5126a8da57ea460/src/scout_apm/django/middleware.py#L68

`request.get_host()` returns the server name, as provided in the `Host` header of the HTTP request. (Actually it's [more complicated](https://docs.djangoproject.com/fr/2.0/ref/request-response/#django.http.HttpRequest.get_host) but that's the general idea.)

You have two solutions to get the IP address:

1. Just use `request.META['REMOTE_ADDR']` and tell users what I'm going to explain below (not necessarily the most user friendly approach).
2. Take the value from `request.META['HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR']` if it's available, fall back to `request.META['REMOTE_ADDR']` if it isn't.

A straightforward way to get the user IP address is `request.META['REMOTE_ADDR']`. It's always there, although I can't find a specification that guarantees that. (I thought it was part of the WSGI spec; apparently not.)

However this fails in the presence of reverse proxies such as an AWS ELB or Heroku's routing layer. There are conventions around the X-Forwarded-For header, but those are tricky to get right — it's extremely easy to create header injection vulnerabilities.

I like option 1 because I think it's up to the developer to determine what their frontmost system is. They could be running on Heroku behind CloudFlare, for example; in that case it's CloudFlare which is authoritative for providing the user IP address. Then they can write a middleware that reads the correct value from the appropriate HTTP header and copies it into request.META['REMOTE_ADDR'].

This middleware needs to run _before_ the Scout middleware if you read the IP address on the "request" side of the middleware. This conflicts with your setup instructions. Looking up the IP address on the "response" side would avoid this problem.

Option 2 is technically incorrect; the proper solution is "I know I'm behind N reverse proxies so I should look for the Nth value from the end of the list" (rather than the first one). Since you aren't selling Scout as a security product, I'm not seeing a critical security concern if an attacker injects an `X-Forwarded-
For` header (which ends up at the beginning of the list and thus causes you to display a bad IP).

That option may be a good tradeoff: many users will get correct IPs out of the box rather than the IP address of the closest reverse proxy (often a private IP address).

----

More generally, you can get the data you need with these APIs: https://docs.djangoproject.com/fr/2.0/ref/request-response/#httprequest-objects

HTTP headers and other metadata provided by the WSGI server is available in `request.META`: https://docs.djangoproject.com/fr/2.0/ref/request-response/#django.http.HttpRequest.META

If you have doubts about the availability or reliability of a particular piece of information, I can investigate.

I agree that relying on UnrecognizedExtension isn't very elegant. What would you suggest changing the return type to? There are really two different functions that need to be able to read out extensions from the certificate. The first is for metadata matching, where an arbitrary OID can be given, and a binary value to match the extensions value against. The second is the transport hints extension defined in the U2F specifications (code for this has not yet been pushed), which reads a bitstring from the extension with OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.45724.2.1.1.

Looking at the source code: ```    def __getattribute__(self, name):
        """Methods that do not return a QuerySet should call ``_filter_visibility`` first."""
        attr = object.__getattribute__(self, name)
        # These methods evaluate the queryset and therefore need to filter the
        # visiblity set.
        evaluation_methods = (
            '_fetch_all', 'count', 'exists', 'aggregate', 'update', '_update',
            'delete', 'undelete',
        )
        if hasattr(attr, '__call__') and name in evaluation_methods:
            def decorator(*args, **kwargs):
                self._filter_visibility()
                return attr(*args, **kwargs)
            return decorator
        return attr```

Removing `_fetch_all` from `evaluation_methods` fixes the issue, but not sure if that breaks anything else.

I was facing similar problem before in Jython. This is how I get it resolved.

First, register a signal handler in your Jython script by:

    import signal
    def intHandler(signum, frame):
        print "Shutting down.."
        System.exit(1)

    # Set the signal handler
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, intHandler)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, intHandler)

This will register the signal handler for the Jython script to handle CTRL+C keyboard input.

However, the default console class org.python.util.JLineConsole treats ctrl+C as a normal character inputs. 

So, Secondly - need to change the python.console to an alternative console class org.python.core.PlainConsole by either change the Jython property:
    
    python.console=org.python.core.PlainConsole

or add the jvm argument:

    -Dpython.console=org.python.core.PlainConsole



This will help you to shutdown the program after CTRL+C is pressed.

It sounds like you haven't set the model in your `settings.py`. You need to set the `STAR_RATINGS_RATING_MODEL` setting. This should be something like:

```
STAR_RATING_RATING_MODEL = '<app_name>.<model_name>
```

Once you have done this all the forms and the admin should use your model instead of the default `Rating` model. Take a look at how django handles the user model because this works in basically tha same way (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/auth/customizing/#substituting-a-custom-user-model)

I would like to point out that `turbodbc` consists of several libraries. Only a supporting library links against `pyarrow`. If `pyarrow` is not present in the environment, `turbodbc` will still work, but with features requiring `pyarrow` being disabled. The user is free to install `pyarrow` at a later time to enable this feature set.

Thus, it is not desirable to force all users of `turbodbc` to have `pyarrow` installed.

@puzrin, YAML 1.2 is explicitly incompatible with JSON on this. It's stated in the spec. Actually, JSON [RFC](http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc4627.txt) also has a statement for key uniqueness, but it says that keys SHOULD be unique (i.e. it's a recommendation for implementation, not a requirement), and YAML spec says that keys MUST be unique.

In my opinion the best thing to do is to restrict duplicate keys globally by default, and add a `json` option to disable this, and bump the minor version number.
Everyone who wish to invent "spec improvements" instead of following standard, can continue in fork. That's out of this project scope.

Thanks @vitalyli, this is a good start. For this to be merged, the test will need to be runnable in Travis. That means it should probably avoid creating new threads wherever possible, since daemon threads can cause Travis to hang. Producing 10000 messages will probably make test runs take much longer than they need to. Additionally, this test only barely verifies that there are no crashes in the code you've written, but since there are components running on separate threads, crashes aren't guaranteed to be visible to Travis.

I suggest moving your test case to [`test_balancedconsumer.py`](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/tests/pykafka/test_balancedconsumer.py), removing the use of threads, having it send only the minimum number of messages necessary to verify that consumers above the partition limit do indeed idle, and adding some `self.assertTrue` calls that ensure that extra consumers are able to start consuming if a space in the group opens up.

Broadly, this is the logic I'd think would be required for a test of this functionality (pseudocode):
```python
# produce some test messages to 2-partition topic

c1 = topic.get_balanced_consumer(group)
c2 = topic.get_balanced_consumer(group)
c3 = topic.get_balanced_consumer(group)

# verify that the first two consumers can consume correctly
m1 = c1.consume()
m2 = c2.consume()
self.assertNotEqual(m1, None)
self.assertNotEqual(m2, None)

# verify that the last consumer doesn't get any messages
m3 = c3.consume()
self.assertEqual(m3, None)

# verify that the last consumer can start consuming if some partitions become available
c2.stop()
m2 = c2.consume()
m3 = c3.consume()
self.assertEqual(m2, None)
self.assertNotEqual(m3, None)
```

This is a high-level view of what I think the test for this change needs to look like.

I just re-discovered this issue and the context for it, related to pull request #348.

So, here is my understanding of the crux of this issue:

- If two consumers are reading, in a balanced manner, from the same topic, and network access to ZK is lost for one of these consumers, if either consumer continues reading messages, it's possible that both consumers will read the same message. This is because a rebalance event could happen before either consumer knows about the change.
- The change @yungchin made here to raise a ZK disconnect exception upon immediate discovery of a downed ZK connection state puts the onus on the person writing a consumer: do you want to sleep until ZK comes online, or do you want to continue consuming messages but risk the possibility that two consumers might read the same message?
- The reason I reverted this change is because the side effect of the change is to make pykafka's typical consumer loop fail in the face of *any* network outage, for even milliseconds, when it seems that the right behavior (for resiliency, anyway) would seem to be to continue consuming messages until ZK comes back online

> BTW another approach might be for the batching bolts to catch that "shut down" signal and clear themselves as part of their shutdown sequence. This might prove simpler, depending of the design of the interface between storm and streamparse.

Currently the only signal we get from Storm is a `SIGKILL` at shutdown time, but that is sent as the topology is actually shutting down, so any batching bolts that need to send things on to other bolts in the topology would fail.  We could probably add a signal handler to catch `SIGKILL` and process the last batch with a warning in the documentation that BatchingBolt should only be used as terminal/final bolt in a topology for exactly this reason.  @kbourgoin, what do you think of that idea?

> You tell me :-). Do we have an underlaying managing layer between storm and streamparse? sorry for the noobish question, I'm not that familiar with streamparse yet..

The only "layer" between Storm and streamparse is the [Multi-Lang Protocol](https://storm.apache.org/documentation/Multilang-protocol.html), which sends JSON messages between the Java ShellBolt/ShellSpout on one side and the streamparse Python implementation on the other side via STDIN/STDOUT.  What would be a nice addition to that protocol would be if there was a "shutdown" message that was sent when a topology was told to shutdown, and that allowed events like this to be triggered.  I've already added stuff to Multi-Lang recently, so I'll put together a PR for that and see what happens.

Thanks, but there is still an issue here in that a Firework can be WAITING even if its parent is FIZZLED or DEFUSED. In that case, it may be WAITING forever, i.e., never become READY, and your rapidfire process will continue forever.

Ideally we'd have a different state for Fireworks that are WAITING behind a READY/RUNNING Firework (still currently expected to run soon) and Fireworks that are WAITING behind a FIZZLED/DEFUSED Firework (may have "gone cold" and never be ready to run unless the user fixes the parent or reignites the parent in the future) but it's not the case at the moment.

If future_run_exists() took that distinction into account it could prevent that "forever waiting for a WAITING firework" issue.

@rhyolight If you can confirm this on Monday, this means that nupic.core now builds on gcc versions 4.9 and 5.x (and the latest Ubuntu LTS version).

@sjmackenzie - we currently bundle the pre-built dependencies for supported platforms. If you were not on linux64 or darwin64 and had Cap'n Proto manually installed then it should work fine. In your case it looks like it is a linux64 build. The error is a bit confusing. Do you have a manually installation of Cap'n Proto?

> So we need to make sure there is an option to force a source install if the binary install isn't working.

Isn't the ability of the user to checkout the code and build manually sufficient?
